name: Blodeuwedd
description: Blodeuwedd, whose name translates to "Flower Face" or "Flower Woman," is a complex and enigmatic figure in Welsh mythology, primarily known from the Fourth Branch of the Mabinogi, *Math fab Mathonwy*. Created by the magicians Math and Gwydion from the blossoms of oak, broom, and meadowsweet, she was fashioned as a wife for Lleu Llaw Gyffes, who was under a curse preventing him from marrying any woman born of woman. Blodeuwedd's story is one of creation, betrayal, and transformation. She represents the power of nature, the artificiality of creation, and the potential for female agency, albeit one that ultimately leads to her downfall and transformation into an owl, a creature of the night. Her narrative is a poignant exploration of fate, free will, and the consequences of defying societal expectations within the context of ancient Welsh beliefs and practices. While her origins are magical, her actions are deeply human, exploring themes of love, desire, and the struggle for autonomy.
subtitle: Flower Face

keyMyths.title: The Creation of Blodeuwedd
keyMyths.description: Math and Gwydion, powerful magicians, created Blodeuwedd from the blossoms of oak, broom, and meadowsweet to fulfill the requirement of providing a wife for Lleu Llaw Gyffes, who was under a curse. This act of creation highlights the magical abilities of Math and Gwydion and the artificial nature of Blodeuwedd's existence. Her very being is tied to the natural world, yet she is not born of it in the traditional sense, setting the stage for her subsequent actions and ultimate transformation.

keyMyths.title: Blodeuwedd's Betrayal
keyMyths.description: Unhappy in her marriage to Lleu, Blodeuwedd falls in love with Gronw Pebr, a lord from Penllyn. Together, they conspire to kill Lleu. Blodeuwedd cunningly extracts the information necessary to circumvent the curse protecting Lleu, learning the specific conditions under which he can be wounded. This act of betrayal underscores the themes of female agency and the lengths to which one will go to pursue forbidden love, while also highlighting the consequences of defying fate and societal expectations.

keyMyths.title: The Transformation into an Owl
keyMyths.description: After Lleu is wounded but not killed, Gwydion finds him and restores him to health. In retaliation for her betrayal, Gwydion transforms Blodeuwedd into an owl, condemning her to a life of hunting at night and being shunned by other birds. This transformation serves as a punishment for her actions and symbolizes her association with darkness and the nocturnal realm. The owl's association with wisdom and prophecy is ironically juxtaposed with Blodeuwedd's deceitful actions.

sources.author: Sioned Davies
sources.description: A modern translation and commentary on the Mabinogion, providing valuable insights into the cultural and historical context of the tales.

sources.author: Miranda Green
sources.work: Myths and Symbols in Pagan Europe
sources.description: Explores the symbolism and religious beliefs of pre-Christian Europe, including Celtic mythology and the significance of figures like Blodeuwedd.

sources.description: Provides a comprehensive overview of Celtic mythology, history, and culture, offering insights into the themes and archetypes present in the Mabinogion.
